A fresh furrow cuts through the tall grass, too straight for any plow. The ground trembles once before the ankheg bursts up beneath the trail.
Laughing calls move through grass high enough to hide the pack. The hyenas test the party from several sides before committing.
A milestone leans beside a wagon rut where the grass has been flattened into hiding places. Raiders rise when the party slows to read it.
The wind makes the plains look like rolling water, erasing all but the largest landmarks. Reading cloud shadow and slope keeps the route true.
A crust of dry earth hides a maze of burrows. A quick step avoids a twisted ankle when the ground gives way.
A blue-black thunderhead builds across half the sky, trailing curtains of rain miles away. The air smells of grass, dust, and lightning.
A lone herd bell rings in the grass without an animal in sight. Tracks nearby suggest rustlers, predators, or both.
A thin line of smoke crawls along the horizon where dry grass has caught fire. Stopping it now may save farms beyond the next rise.
A weathered marker has been knocked over in a field of rusted spearheads. Restoring it reveals a name someone recently tried to erase.
A stand of wild grain bends heavy with seed beside a shallow swale. Cutting it before the wind scatters the heads makes a simple meal.
Purple flowers mark a patch of wild onions under thin soil. Pulling them carefully keeps the bulbs whole.
White yarrow blooms across a low ridge where the grass grows short. The useful heads must be clipped before insects strip them.
A muddy stock pond flashes with bluegill beneath dragonflies. Casting from the firm bank avoids sinking knee-deep in churned mud.
A slow creek bend hides catfish under a cut bank. The water is shallow, but the bank crumbles when stepped on too hard.
Last night's rain filled a wheel-rut pool with trapped minnows from the flooded ditch. The opportunity will be gone by noon.
Sharp flint nodules poke through the soil on a low rise. Sorting good cores from cracked stone takes a practiced eye.
A creek has exposed clean clay below the grass roots. The bank is low but slick, and careless digging clouds the water.
Rust-colored water gathers where the plain dips into a marshy hollow. Iron nodules can be lifted from the muck before it swallows tools.
A cottonwood has fallen beside a seasonal creek, dry on top and damp below. The straight sections make light, workable timber.
An abandoned fence line sags across the grass, its posts silvered by weather but still solid. Pulling them cleanly saves hours of cutting.
Willows crowd a shallow wash where their flexible shoots can be cut for baskets and bindings. The best shoots grow over slick mud.
